selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":42,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"asexual","occupation":"delivery driver","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"lower secondary","parent_2":"lower secondary"},"current_marital_status":"in a relationship","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"normal","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["running","volunteering","photography","reading novels","rock climbing"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":18,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":76,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":21,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":52,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":93,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":42,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"asexual","occupation":"delivery driver","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"lower secondary","parent_2":"lower secondary"},"current_marital_status":"in a relationship","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"normal","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["running","volunteering","photography","reading novels","rock climbing"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":18,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":76,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":21,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":52,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":93,"level":"high"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I think the best way is probably not even to look at it, or maybe just get rid of that feed entirely. I'm a bit overwhelmed by all this stuff anyway — my main thing these days is driving deliveries around Philadelphia and trying to keep my head down.

Honestly, seeing fake content makes me feel worse about things like misinformation spreading online. But with my education level being upper secondary, not exactly the kind that helps decode social media conspiracies every day.

Maybe there's a way we can just... ignore it? Or flag it somehow so algorithms pick it up and filter it out. I mean, look at how much agreeableness is in fake news — often they're designed to be catchy or emotional but not really true.

As an atheist who grew up with both parents having lower secondary education, I don't have any special background here. Just someone dealing with the daily grind of work and family life.

I spend only a few times monthly on social media now — too many distractions when you're trying to make ends meet as a delivery driver. But still, sometimes people post stuff that feels fake.

I'm not sure I want to deal with it more than I already do; my hobbies are things like running and rock climbing which keep me grounded away from screens.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Considering the persona's low openness (18) meaning they're probably not interested in deep dives into complex topics, their high conscientiousness (76) might suggest a structured approach to filtering content. But with moderate agreeableness (52), there could be some emotional response involved.

Their education level being upper secondary makes them likely less equipped for verifying nuanced information online. Their native-born American background and normal psychological features don't directly affect this, but their high neuroticism score (93) might make them more sensitive to the stress of fake content.",
  "tone": "Pragmatic with a hint of frustration",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "gender", "sexual_orientation", "occupation", "city_of_living", "employment_status", "education_level", "parents_education", "current_marital_status", "children", "migration_status", "psychological_feature", "religious_beliefs", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think the best way is probably not even to look at it, or maybe just get rid of that feed entirely. I'm a bit overwhelmed by all this stuff anyway — my main thing these days is driving deliveries around Philadelphia and trying to keep my head down.

Honestly, seeing fake content makes me feel worse about things like misinformation spreading online. But with my education level being upper secondary, not exactly the kind that helps decode social media conspiracies every day.

Maybe there's a way we can just... ignore it? Or flag it somehow so algorithms pick it up and filter it out. I mean, look at how much agreeableness is in fake news — often they're designed to be catchy or emotional but not really true.

As an atheist who grew up with both parents having lower secondary education, I don't have any special background here. Just someone dealing with the daily grind of work and family life.

I spend only a few times monthly on social media now — too many distractions when you're trying to make ends meet as a delivery driver. But still, sometimes people post stuff that feels fake.

I'm not sure I want to deal with it more than I already do; my hobbies are things like running and rock climbing which keep me grounded away from screens.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
Considering the persona's low openness (18) meaning they're probably not interested in deep dives into complex topics, their high conscientiousness (76) might suggest a structured approach to filtering content. But with moderate agreeableness (52), there could be some emotional response involved.

Their education level being upper secondary makes them likely less equipped for verifying nuanced information online. Their native-born American background and normal psychological features don't directly affect this, but their high neuroticism score (93) might make them more sensitive to the stress of fake content.
response_parsed.tone: Pragmatic with a hint of frustration
